1:
"Moreover, brethren, I would not that ye should be ignorant, how that all our fathers were under the cloud, and all passed through the sea;" The witnesses note the privileges of the fathers under the cloud and through the sea. On "all our fathers were under the cloud," the grammatical voices note the favour. Barnes and Robertson note Paul reminds the Corinthians that all the Israelites ("our fathers") enjoyed great privileges — all were under the cloud (the pillar of cloud, the symbol of God's guiding presence, Exodus 13:21).

On "all passed through the sea," the witnesses note the deliverance. Barnes notes all passed through the Red Sea on dry ground — a signal token of divine protection and favour, shared by the whole nation.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the purpose: Paul recounts these high privileges to warn the Corinthians — for though all Israel shared them, most perished; great privileges are no guarantee of safety. The verse displays the privileges of the fathers under the cloud and through the sea.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul reminds the Corinthians that all the Israelites enjoyed great privileges — all under the cloud (the pillar, the symbol of God's guiding presence) and all passing through the Red Sea on dry ground, tokens of divine protection shared by the whole nation (Barnes, Robertson). Henry and Calvin note Paul recounts these to warn the Corinthians: though all Israel shared them, most perished, so privileges are no guarantee of safety. The verse displays the privileges of the fathers. The verse marks the cloud: God's presence. The verse displays the sea: the deliverance. The verse displays the universality: all the fathers. The verse displays the purpose: a warning. The privileges display the high favour shared by all Israel. The verse displays the cloud: guiding. The verse displays the sea: passed through. The verse displays the warning: to come. The privileges of the fathers under the cloud and through the sea display the high favour God showed all Israel: every one of them enjoyed the guiding presence and the deliverance — privileges Paul recounts to warn the over-confident Corinthians that such favours did not preserve the nation. The verse opens the wilderness example as a warning, and likens these privileges to Christian sacraments (vv.2-4).
2:
"And were all baptized unto Moses in the cloud and in the sea;" The brief verse notes the fathers baptized unto Moses in the cloud and sea. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul represents the Israelites as "baptized unto Moses" — that is, by passing under the cloud and through the sea, they were initiated into the covenant under Moses's leadership, a figure analogous to Christian baptism into Christ.

On "in the cloud and in the sea," the witnesses note the elements. Barnes notes the cloud above and the sea around them were the media of this symbolic baptism — they were as it were enveloped, dedicated to Moses as their leader and to the covenant.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the parallel: as the Israelites were baptized unto Moses, so Christians are baptized unto Christ; the privilege corresponds, and so does the warning. The verse displays the fathers baptized unto Moses in the cloud and sea.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul represents the Israelites as "baptized unto Moses" — by passing under the cloud and through the sea they were initiated into the covenant under Moses's leadership, a figure analogous to Christian baptism (Barnes, Robertson); the cloud above and the sea around the media of this symbolic baptism, dedicating them to Moses and the covenant (Barnes). Wesley notes the parallel: as Israel was baptized unto Moses, so Christians unto Christ. The verse displays the fathers baptized unto Moses. The verse marks the figure: baptized unto Moses. The verse displays the elements: cloud and sea. The verse displays the dedication: to Moses. The verse displays the parallel: with Christian baptism. The baptism displays Israel's initiation under Moses paralleling Christian baptism. The verse displays the cloud: above. The verse displays the sea: around. The verse displays the parallel: unto Christ. The fathers baptized unto Moses in the cloud and sea display Israel's initiation into the covenant under Moses, a figure that parallels Christian baptism: as they were dedicated to Moses by passing under the cloud and through the sea, so believers are baptized unto Christ. The verse displays the sacramental likeness between Israel and the church, continued in their spiritual food (v.3).
3:
"And did all eat the same spiritual meat;" The brief verse notes the fathers eating the same spiritual meat. The grammatical voices note the manna. Barnes and Robertson note the "spiritual meat" is the manna (Exodus 16) — called "spiritual" not because of any peculiar nature but because it was supernaturally given, a token of divine favour, and (as Paul develops) a type pointing to Christ.

On "the same," the witnesses note the universality. Barnes notes all the Israelites alike ate this miraculous food — a common privilege of the whole nation, as the Lord's Supper is common to all Christians.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the typology: the manna was a figure of Christ, the true bread from heaven (John 6:31-33); the fathers fed, in their measure, on the same Christ believers now feed on. The verse displays the fathers eating the same spiritual meat.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the "spiritual meat" is the manna (Exodus 16), called "spiritual" not for any peculiar nature but because supernaturally given, a token of divine favour and a type pointing to Christ (Barnes, Robertson); all the Israelites alike ate this miraculous food, a common privilege of the nation (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the manna was a figure of Christ, the true bread from heaven (John 6); the fathers fed in their measure on the same Christ. The verse displays the fathers eating spiritual meat. The verse marks the meat: the manna. The verse displays the universality: all ate. The verse displays the type: of Christ. The verse displays the parallel: the Lord's Supper. The spiritual meat displays the manna typifying Christ. The verse displays the manna: supernatural. The verse displays the sharing: all alike. The verse displays the type: Christ the bread. The fathers eating the same spiritual meat displays the supernatural food that typified Christ: all Israel alike fed on the manna, the miraculous bread of heaven that pointed to Christ, the true bread — a privilege common to the whole nation, as the Lord's Supper is to the church. The verse displays the typical feeding of Israel on Christ, continued in their spiritual drink (v.4).
4:
"And did all drink the same spiritual drink: for they drank of that spiritual Rock that followed them: and that Rock was Christ." The witnesses note the spiritual drink from the Rock, and the Rock that was Christ — the pre-existence crux. On "the same spiritual drink... of that spiritual Rock that followed them," the grammatical voices note the supply. Barnes and Robertson note the "spiritual drink" is the water miraculously given from the rock (Exodus 17:6; Numbers 20:11); "the Rock that followed them" means the stream of water that accompanied Israel in their journeyings — not (literally) the rock itself moving, though Paul may adapt a rabbinic legend of the following rock.

On "that Rock was Christ," the great Christological question arises. Many (Robertson, Calvin, Clarke, Cyril, Ellicott) take it of the pre-existent Christ: Christ himself was the spiritual Rock, the source of the water and the abiding presence with Israel — a testimony to his pre-existence and deity (Cyril: it was Christ whom Moses saw). The grammatical-historical Barnes reads "was" as "represented": the rock was a vivid emblem of Christ and his grace, not the pre-incarnate Christ literally following.

The witnesses note the sacramental sense: Calvin holds Christ was joined to the sign sacramentally, the fathers truly partaking of him by the Spirit. The verse displays the spiritual drink from the Rock that was Christ, with the pre-existence crux.

The witnesses agree the "spiritual drink" is the water miraculously given from the rock (Exodus 17; Numbers 20), "the Rock that followed them" meaning the stream that accompanied Israel (Barnes, Robertson), possibly with allusion to a rabbinic legend. But "that Rock was Christ" divides them: Robertson, Calvin, Clarke, Cyril, and Ellicott take it of the pre-existent Christ — the spiritual Rock, source of the water and Israel's abiding presence, a testimony to his pre-existence and deity; Barnes reads "was" as "represented," the rock a vivid emblem of Christ, not the pre-incarnate Christ literally following. Calvin adds the sacramental sense: Christ joined to the sign, the fathers partaking of him by the Spirit. The verse displays the Rock that was Christ. The verse marks the drink: water from the rock. The verse displays the Rock: that followed. The verse displays the debate: pre-existent Christ or emblem. The verse displays the sacrament: Christ joined to the sign. The Rock displays Christ as the source of Israel's spiritual supply. The verse displays the water: supernatural. The verse displays the Rock: Christ. The verse displays the crux: pre-existence. The spiritual drink from the Rock that was Christ displays Christ as the source of Israel's supernatural supply: the water from the rock, accompanying them in the wilderness, flowed from Christ — read by most as the pre-existent Christ himself present with Israel (his deity), and by Barnes as the rock representing Christ. The verse displays the fathers' partaking of Christ under the old covenant, and turns to their fall despite these privileges (v.5).
5:
"But with many of them God was not well pleased: for they were overthrown in the wilderness." The witnesses note that despite their privileges, most of the fathers perished. On "with many of them God was not well pleased," the grammatical voices note the displeasure. Barnes and Robertson note the sharp contrast ("but"): notwithstanding all their privileges (vv.1-4), God was not pleased with the greater part of them — their sin provoked his displeasure.

On "they were overthrown in the wilderness," the witnesses note the judgment. Barnes notes "overthrown" (strewn down, scattered as corpses) — the vast majority fell in the wilderness and never entered Canaan, their carcasses strewing the desert (Numbers 14; Hebrews 3:17).

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the lesson: privileges and sacraments did not save the unfaithful; the Corinthians, trusting their own privileges, must take warning from Israel's overthrow. The verse displays that despite their privileges, most of the fathers perished.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: notwithstanding all their privileges, God was not pleased with the greater part of them, their sin provoking his displeasure (Barnes, Robertson); "overthrown" (strewn down as corpses) — the majority fell in the wilderness and never entered Canaan (Numbers 14; Hebrews 3:17, Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note privileges and sacraments did not save the unfaithful; the Corinthians must take warning. The verse displays the fathers' fall. The verse marks the displeasure: God not pleased. The verse displays the judgment: overthrown in the wilderness. The verse displays the contrast: privileges yet perished. The verse displays the lesson: a warning. The fall displays that privileges did not preserve the unfaithful. The verse displays the displeasure: with many. The verse displays the overthrow: in the desert. The verse displays the warning: to the Corinthians. That despite their privileges most of the fathers perished displays the sobering truth that sacraments and favours do not save the unfaithful: though all Israel shared the cloud, the sea, the manna, and the Rock, God was displeased with most, and they were overthrown in the wilderness. The verse displays the great warning against presumption, and draws the lesson explicitly (v.6).
6:
"Now these things were our examples, to the intent we should not lust after evil things, as they also lusted." The witnesses note that Israel's fall is a warning example to us. On "these things were our examples," the grammatical voices note the typology. Barnes and Robertson note "examples" (typoi, types, patterns) — Israel's history was written as a warning pattern for Christians; their experiences typify the dangers and judgments believers must avoid.

On "we should not lust after evil things, as they also lusted," the witnesses note the first warning. Barnes notes the first lesson: we are not to lust (crave evil things) as the Israelites did, who craved the fleshpots of Egypt and despised God's provision (Numbers 11).

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the purpose: God recorded Israel's sins and judgments precisely to admonish later generations; the Corinthians (and we) are to read them as warnings against the same evil desires. The verse displays Israel's fall as a warning example to us.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "examples" (typoi, types, patterns) — Israel's history was written as a warning pattern for Christians, their experiences typifying the dangers and judgments believers must avoid (Barnes, Robertson); the first lesson being not to lust (crave evil things) as the Israelites did, who craved Egypt's fleshpots and despised God's provision (Numbers 11, Barnes). Henry and Calvin note God recorded Israel's sins to admonish later generations. The verse displays Israel's fall as a warning. The verse marks the typology: our examples. The verse displays the first warning: not to lust. The verse displays the pattern: Israel's craving. The verse displays the purpose: admonition. The examples display Israel's history as a warning pattern. The verse displays the types: for us. The verse displays the lust: to be avoided. The verse displays the warning: against evil desire. That Israel's fall is a warning example to us displays the admonitory purpose of their history: their experiences were recorded as types and patterns, that Christians should not crave evil things as Israel did. The verse displays the typical function of the wilderness narrative, and lists the specific sins to be shunned (vv.7-10).
7:
"Neither be ye idolaters, as were some of them; as it is written, The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play." The witnesses note the warning against idolatry. On "neither be ye idolaters, as were some of them," the grammatical voices note the second warning. Barnes and Robertson note the second sin to be shunned: idolatry — as some of the Israelites became idolaters, worshipping the golden calf (Exodus 32); this warning bears directly on the Corinthian danger of idol-feasts.

On the citation, the witnesses note the description. Barnes notes Paul quotes Exodus 32:6: "the people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play" — the feasting and revelry that accompanied the calf-worship, the eating of the idol-feast and the licentious sport that followed.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the application: the idol-feasts at Corinth, with their eating and revelry, were of a piece with Israel's idolatry; Paul warns the Corinthians not to repeat it. The verse displays the warning against idolatry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second sin to be shunned is idolatry — as some Israelites worshipped the golden calf (Exodus 32), a warning bearing on the Corinthian danger of idol-feasts (Barnes, Robertson); Paul quoting Exodus 32:6, "the people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play," the feasting and revelry of the calf-worship (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the Corinthian idol-feasts were of a piece with Israel's idolatry. The verse displays the warning against idolatry. The verse marks the sin: idolatry. The verse displays the citation: eat, drink, play. The verse displays the parallel: the idol-feasts. The verse displays the application: to Corinth. The warning displays idolatry as the chief peril at Corinth. The verse displays the calf: Israel's idol. The verse displays the feasting: and revelry. The verse displays the danger: at Corinth. The warning against idolatry displays the chief peril Paul addresses: as Israel fell into calf-worship with its feasting and revelry, so the Corinthians, frequenting idol-feasts, are warned not to become idolaters. The verse displays the central danger of the chapter, and adds the warning against fornication (v.8).
8:
"Neither let us commit fornication, as some of them committed, and fell in one day three and twenty thousand." The witnesses note the warning against fornication. On "neither let us commit fornication, as some of them committed," the grammatical voices note the third warning. Barnes and Robertson note the third sin: fornication — as some Israelites did at Baal-peor, joining idolatry to immorality with the daughters of Moab (Numbers 25); a sin to which Corinth was especially prone.

On "fell in one day three and twenty thousand," the witnesses note the judgment — and a numerical question. Barnes and Lightfoot note Numbers 25:9 gives twenty-four thousand; Paul says twenty-three thousand "in one day." Most reconcile it: either Paul gives the number that fell in the single day of the plague (the rest, to twenty-four thousand, dying by the judges or on following days), or rounds the figure.

The Reformed Calvin notes the lesson: the swift and terrible judgment on Israel's fornication warns the Corinthians against the immorality bound up with their idolatry. The verse displays the warning against fornication.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the third sin is fornication — as some Israelites did at Baal-peor, joining idolatry to immorality (Numbers 25), a sin to which Corinth was prone (Barnes, Robertson); and "fell in one day three and twenty thousand" — Numbers 25:9 gives twenty-four thousand, reconciled by taking Paul's figure as those who fell in the single day of the plague, or a rounding (Barnes, Lightfoot). Calvin notes the swift judgment warns the Corinthians. The verse displays the warning against fornication. The verse marks the sin: fornication. The verse displays the judgment: 23,000 in a day. The verse displays the question: the number. The verse displays the lesson: swift judgment. The warning displays fornication's terrible judgment. The verse displays the immorality: at Baal-peor. The verse displays the fall: in one day. The verse displays the danger: at Corinth. The warning against fornication displays the swift and terrible judgment on immorality: as Israel fell at Baal-peor, twenty-three thousand perishing in a day, so the Corinthians are warned against the fornication bound up with their idolatry. The verse displays the deadly link of idolatry and immorality, and adds the warning against tempting Christ (v.9).
9:
"Neither let us tempt Christ, as some of them also tempted, and were destroyed of serpents." The witnesses note the warning against tempting Christ. On "neither let us tempt Christ," the grammatical voices note the fourth warning — and a Christological point. Barnes and Robertson note the fourth sin: tempting Christ (provoking, putting to the test) — as the Israelites tempted God in the wilderness (Numbers 21:5-6); the reading "Christ" (in many manuscripts) implies that Christ was present with Israel and was the one they tempted, a testimony to his pre-existence (cf. v.4).

On "were destroyed of serpents," the witnesses note the judgment. Barnes notes the fiery serpents sent among the people (Numbers 21:6) as punishment for their murmuring and testing of God — many perished.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the lesson: to tempt Christ is to presume upon his patience and provoke his judgment; the Corinthians, by their license, were in danger of doing the same. The verse displays the warning against tempting Christ.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fourth sin is tempting Christ (provoking, putting to the test) — as Israel tempted God in the wilderness (Numbers 21); the reading "Christ" implying he was present with Israel and was the one tempted, a testimony to his pre-existence (cf. v.4, Barnes, Robertson); and "destroyed of serpents," the fiery serpents sent as punishment (Numbers 21:6, Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note to tempt Christ is to presume on his patience and provoke judgment, a danger to the licentious Corinthians. The verse displays the warning against tempting Christ. The verse marks the sin: tempting Christ. The verse displays the judgment: destroyed of serpents. The verse displays the Christology: Christ present with Israel. The verse displays the lesson: presuming on his patience. The warning displays the peril of provoking Christ. The verse displays the tempting: of Christ. The verse displays the serpents: the judgment. The verse displays the presence: of Christ. The warning against tempting Christ displays the peril of provoking him: as Israel tempted God (Christ present with them) and were destroyed by serpents, so the Corinthians, presuming on Christ's patience by their license, are warned not to provoke his judgment. The verse displays Christ's presence with Israel and the danger of provoking him, and adds the warning against murmuring (v.10).
10:
"Neither murmur ye, as some of them also murmured, and were destroyed of the destroyer." The brief verse notes the warning against murmuring. The grammatical voices note the fifth warning. Barnes and Robertson note the fifth and last sin: murmuring (complaining, discontent against God and his appointed leaders) — as the Israelites murmured (Numbers 14; 16), against Moses and against God's dealings.

On "destroyed of the destroyer," the witnesses note the judgment. Barnes notes "the destroyer" is the destroying angel (or the agent of God's judgment) who smote the murmurers — as in the plague after Korah's rebellion (Numbers 16:41-49), or the destroying angel generally.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the application: murmuring against God's providence and his ministers brought destruction on Israel; the Corinthians, murmuring against Paul and chafing at restraint, are warned. The verse displays the warning against murmuring.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fifth and last sin is murmuring (complaining against God and his leaders) — as Israel murmured (Numbers 14; 16) against Moses and God's dealings (Barnes, Robertson); "the destroyer" being the destroying angel or agent of God's judgment who smote the murmurers (Numbers 16:41-49, Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note murmuring against God's providence and ministers brought destruction; the Corinthians, chafing at restraint, are warned. The verse displays the warning against murmuring. The verse marks the sin: murmuring. The verse displays the judgment: the destroyer. The verse displays the parallel: Israel's complaints. The verse displays the application: to Corinth. The warning displays the peril of murmuring against God. The verse displays the complaining: against God. The verse displays the destroyer: the judgment. The verse displays the danger: to the Corinthians. The warning against murmuring displays the peril of discontent against God: as Israel murmured against Moses and God's dealings and were smitten by the destroyer, so the Corinthians, murmuring against their apostle and chafing at restraint, are warned. The verse displays the last of the five wilderness sins, and states the abiding purpose of these warnings (v.11). The sin seems the lightest of the five, a mere murmur of discontent, yet it drew down a destruction as real as any; and in this the apostle teaches that the heart which chafes against God and his appointed ministers is already far advanced toward the open rebellion that ruins the soul.
11:
"Now all these things happened unto them for ensamples: and they are written for our admonition, upon whom the ends of the world are come." The witnesses note that Israel's history was written for our admonition. On "all these things happened unto them for ensamples," the grammatical voices note the typology. Barnes and Robertson note Paul repeats that these events befell Israel "for ensamples" (typoi, types) — designed as patterns and warnings, not mere history.

On "written for our admonition," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note they were recorded in Scripture for our admonition (instruction, warning) — Christians being the intended beneficiaries of these recorded judgments.

On "upon whom the ends of the world are come," the witnesses note the present age. Barnes and Robertson note Christians live in "the ends of the ages" — the last dispensation, the close of the world's epochs, the gospel age toward which all former ages pointed; the accumulated warnings of history press upon them with peculiar force. The verse displays that Israel's history was written for our admonition.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul repeats that these events befell Israel "for ensamples" (typoi, types), designed as patterns and warnings, not mere history (Barnes, Robertson); recorded in Scripture for our admonition, Christians being the intended beneficiaries (Barnes, Calvin); who live in "the ends of the ages" — the last dispensation, the gospel age toward which all former ages pointed, the warnings pressing with peculiar force (Barnes, Robertson). The verse displays Israel's history written for us. The verse marks the typology: for ensamples. The verse displays the purpose: our admonition. The verse displays the age: the ends of the world. The verse displays the force: peculiar urgency. The history displays the warnings written for the last age. The verse displays the types: patterns. The verse displays the admonition: for us. The verse displays the age: the last. That Israel's history was written for our admonition displays the abiding purpose of the wilderness judgments: they befell Israel as types and were recorded for the warning of Christians, who live in the last dispensation upon whom the ends of the ages have come. The verse displays the urgent application of these examples to the present age, and draws the personal warning (v.12).
12:
"Wherefore let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall." The witnesses note the warning against self-confidence — the perseverance crux. On "let him that thinketh he standeth," the grammatical voices note the presumption. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note the practical conclusion: the one who supposes himself secure (firm in faith, beyond danger) — and Clarke notes "thinketh" strengthens rather than weakens the sense (he who most confidently stands) — is precisely the one in peril.

On "take heed lest he fall," the great debate divides. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads it as a real possibility of final apostasy: the highest saint may fall, "not only partially but finally," for in a state of probation everything may change while one depends on God. The Reformed Calvin reads it as a warning against carnal security, not the assurance of faith: Paul reproves the puffed-up self-confidence of the flesh, not the sacred assurance that rests on God's promises; the elect are humbled and so kept, but do not finally fall.

The grammatical-historical Barnes notes the practical point both share: confidence in one's own security is no evidence of safety, and may be the strongest evidence of danger; all are to take heed. The verse displays the warning against self-confidence, with the perseverance crux.

The witnesses agree the practical point: the one who supposes himself secure is precisely in peril; confidence in one's own security is no evidence of safety, and all are to take heed (Barnes, Clarke, Robertson — Clarke noting "thinketh" strengthens the sense). But the warning divides them: Clarke reads it as a real possibility of final apostasy (the highest saint may fall, not only partially but finally, in a state of probation); Calvin reads it as a warning against carnal self-confidence, not the assurance of faith — Paul reproving the puffed-up security of the flesh, the elect being humbled and so kept, not finally falling. The verse displays the warning against self-confidence. The verse marks the presumption: thinketh he standeth. The verse displays the peril: lest he fall. The verse displays the debate: final apostasy or carnal security. The verse displays the point: self-distrust. The warning displays the danger of presumption and the perseverance crux. The verse displays the standing: presumed. The verse displays the falling: feared. The verse displays the crux: perseverance. The warning against self-confidence — let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall — displays both the universal call to self-distrust and the perseverance crux: read by the Arminian as a real possibility that a true believer may finally fall, and by the Reformed as a rebuke of carnal security that the elect heed as a means of being kept. The verse displays the solemn lesson of Israel's overthrow, and answers the fear it raises with God's faithfulness (v.13).
13:
"There hath no temptation taken you but such as is common to man: but God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able; but will with the temptation also make a way to escape, that ye may be able to bear it." The witnesses note God's faithfulness in providing a way of escape from temptation. On "no temptation but such as is common to man," the grammatical voices note the comfort. Barnes and Robertson note Paul reassures: the temptations (trials, tests) they face are only "common to man" — such as all men experience, not beyond human endurance, nothing strange or singular.

On "God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that ye are able," the witnesses note the divine fidelity. Barnes and Calvin note the ground of confidence: God is faithful, and will not permit them to be tempted beyond their strength — he measures the trial to the strength he gives.

On "with the temptation also make a way to escape," the eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the promise: with every temptation God provides "a way of escape" (a way out), that they may be able to bear it — not always removal, but strength and a path through. The verse displays God's faithfulness in providing a way of escape.

The witnesses agree: Paul reassures that the temptations they face are only "common to man" — such as all experience, not beyond human endurance (Barnes, Robertson); the ground of confidence being God's faithfulness, who will not permit them to be tempted beyond their strength, measuring the trial to the strength he gives (Barnes, Calvin); and with every temptation he provides "a way of escape," that they may bear it — not always removal but strength and a path through (Chrysostom, Calvin). The verse displays God's faithfulness in temptation. The verse marks the comfort: common to man. The verse displays the fidelity: God faithful. The verse displays the limit: not above what ye are able. The verse displays the promise: a way to escape. God's faithfulness displays the sure provision against temptation. The verse displays the trial: common. The verse displays the keeping: within strength. The verse displays the escape: provided. God's faithfulness in providing a way of escape from temptation displays the believer's sure comfort: no trial is beyond what is common to man, and the faithful God will not let them be tempted beyond their strength, but with every temptation makes a way to bear and escape it. The verse displays the divine fidelity that answers the fear of falling, and returns to the urgent command against idolatry (v.14).
14:
"Wherefore, my dearly beloved, flee from idolatry." The brief verse notes the command to flee idolatry. The grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws the practical conclusion from all the foregoing (the wilderness warnings, the faithfulness of God): "flee from idolatry" — not merely avoid it, but flee (run from, shun utterly) every form of it, including the idol-feasts.

On "my dearly beloved," the witnesses note the tenderness. Barnes notes the affectionate address — Paul's warning flows from love, not harshness; he urges the flight from idolatry as one who seeks their good.

The Reformed Calvin notes the force: idolatry is so deadly that the only safe course is flight; one is not to dally with it or test how near one may come, but to flee. The verse displays the command to flee idolatry.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul draws the practical conclusion from all the foregoing — "flee from idolatry," not merely avoid but flee (shun utterly) every form of it, including the idol-feasts (Barnes, Robertson); the affectionate address ("my dearly beloved") showing the warning flows from love, not harshness (Barnes). Calvin notes idolatry is so deadly that the only safe course is flight, not dallying or testing how near one may come. The verse displays the command to flee idolatry. The verse marks the command: flee idolatry. The verse displays the tenderness: dearly beloved. The verse displays the force: shun utterly. The verse displays the wisdom: flight, not dallying. The command displays idolatry to be fled, not flirted with. The verse displays the flight: from idolatry. The verse displays the love: behind the warning. The verse displays the urgency: utter avoidance. The command to flee idolatry displays the only safe course toward so deadly a sin: Paul, in tender love, urges the Corinthians not merely to avoid but to flee idolatry in every form — not testing how near they may safely come, but running from it. The verse displays the decisive exhortation that governs the idol-feast question, and appeals to their own judgment (v.15).
15:
"I speak as to wise men; judge ye what I say." The brief verse notes Paul appealing to their own judgment. The grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul addresses the Corinthians as "wise men" — those able to weigh an argument — and invites them to "judge" (discern, evaluate) for themselves what he is about to say about the Lord's Supper and idol-feasts.

On the rhetorical force, the witnesses note the respect and confidence. Barnes notes Paul appeals to their own reason and sense, confident that, if they weigh the matter fairly, they will see the truth of his point — a respectful, persuasive approach.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the implication: the argument he is about to make (vv.16-22) is so clear that their own wisdom, fairly exercised, will confirm it; he does not dictate but reasons. The verse displays Paul appealing to their own judgment.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul addresses the Corinthians as "wise men" — those able to weigh an argument — and invites them to "judge" (discern, evaluate) for themselves what he is about to say (Barnes, Robertson); appealing to their own reason and sense, confident that if they weigh the matter fairly they will see the truth, a respectful and persuasive approach (Barnes). Henry and Calvin note the coming argument is so clear that their own wisdom will confirm it; he reasons rather than dictates. The verse displays Paul appealing to their judgment. The verse marks the address: as to wise men. The verse displays the invitation: judge ye. The verse displays the respect: their own reason. The verse displays the confidence: the truth will appear. The appeal displays Paul reasoning with their own discernment. The verse displays the wisdom: ascribed to them. The verse displays the judging: invited. The verse displays the clarity: of the argument. Paul appealing to their own judgment displays his respectful, persuasive method: addressing the Corinthians as wise men, he invites them to weigh his argument for themselves, confident that their own discernment will confirm the truth of what he says about the Lord's table and idolatry. The verse displays the apostle's appeal to reason, and presents the argument from the Lord's Supper (vv.16-17).
16:
"The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not the communion of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not the communion of the body of Christ?" The witnesses note the communion of Christ's blood and body in the Supper — the eucharistic crux. On "the cup of blessing which we bless," the grammatical voices note the cup. Barnes and Robertson note "the cup of blessing" is the eucharistic cup, so called because over it God is blessed and praised for redemption (the Jewish "cup of blessing"); "which we bless" means it is set apart by thanksgiving to a sacred use, no physical change implied.

On "the communion of the blood of Christ... the body of Christ," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical Barnes reads "communion" (koinonia) as the participation by which believers share alike in the benefits of Christ's blood and body and express their union with him and one another — the cup and bread emblems exhibiting this. The Reformed Calvin reads it as a real spiritual participation: the figure is not empty, the reality present, the soul truly partaking of Christ's blood as the mouth drinks the wine — a spiritual real presence, against both an empty memorial and the Papist transubstantiation.

The witnesses note the shared ground: the koinonia is a genuine participation in Christ, not a mere sign; Robertson calls it "a spiritual participation in the blood of Christ symbolized by the cup." The verse displays the communion of Christ's blood and body in the Supper.

The witnesses agree "the cup of blessing" is the eucharistic cup (so called because over it God is blessed for redemption), set apart by thanksgiving, no physical change implied (Barnes, Robertson); and that the "communion" (koinonia) is a genuine participation in Christ, not a mere sign — "a spiritual participation in the blood of Christ symbolized by the cup" (Robertson). But its nature divides them: Barnes reads it as sharing alike in the benefits of Christ's blood and body and expressing union with him and one another, the cup and bread emblems; Calvin reads it as a real spiritual participation, the soul truly partaking of Christ's blood as the mouth drinks the wine — a spiritual real presence, against both empty memorialism and transubstantiation. The verse displays the communion in the Supper. The verse marks the cup: the communion of the blood. The verse displays the bread: the communion of the body. The verse displays the debate: spiritual participation or emblem of union. The verse displays the core: a genuine partaking. The communion displays the believer's real participation in Christ. The verse displays the cup: blessed. The verse displays the koinonia: participation. The verse displays the crux: the eucharistic presence. The communion of Christ's blood and body in the Supper displays the believer's genuine participation in Christ: the cup and bread are the communion of his blood and body — read by the grammatical-historical as sharing alike in his benefits and union with him, and by Calvin as a real spiritual partaking of Christ, the figure not empty but the reality present. The verse displays the union with Christ in the Supper, and draws from it the unity of the church (v.17).
17:
"For we being many are one bread, and one body: for we are all partakers of that one bread." The witnesses note the church's unity in the one bread and body. On "we being many are one bread, and one body," the grammatical voices note the unity. Barnes and Robertson note Paul draws from the Supper the unity of the church: though believers are many, they are "one bread" (one loaf) and "one body" — united into a single body by their common partaking.

On "we are all partakers of that one bread," the witnesses note the ground. Barnes notes the shared participation in the one loaf is both the sign and the seal of their union — all eating of the one bread, they are made and shown to be one body in Christ.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the application: the Supper binds believers into one body in Christ; therefore to partake also at the table of idols would be to rend that union and join themselves to idolaters. The verse displays the church's unity in the one bread and body.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul draws from the Supper the unity of the church — though many, believers are "one bread" (one loaf) and "one body," united by their common partaking (Barnes, Robertson); the shared participation in the one loaf being both sign and seal of their union, all eating of the one bread and so made one body in Christ (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the Supper binds believers into one body, so partaking at the idol-table would rend that union. The verse displays the church's unity. The verse marks the unity: one bread, one body. The verse displays the ground: all partakers. The verse displays the union: in Christ. The verse displays the application: against idol-feasts. The unity displays the church made one by the common partaking. The verse displays the many: made one. The verse displays the bread: one loaf. The verse displays the body: one in Christ. The church's unity in the one bread and body displays the binding effect of the Supper: though many, believers become and are shown to be one body by all partaking of the one loaf — a union that would be violated by joining also at the table of idols. The verse displays the corporate union effected in the Supper, and confirms the principle from Israel's altar (v.18).
18:
"Behold Israel after the flesh: are not they which eat of the sacrifices partakers of the altar?" The witnesses note the argument from Israel's altar: eating the sacrifice unites to the altar. On "Israel after the flesh," the grammatical voices note the example. Barnes and Robertson note Paul points to "Israel after the flesh" (the natural Israel, the Jewish nation in their temple worship) as a parallel case to illustrate his principle.

On "they which eat of the sacrifices, partakers of the altar," the witnesses note the principle. Barnes and Calvin note the point: those who ate of the Jewish sacrifices were thereby "partakers of the altar" — joined in fellowship with the altar and the God worshipped there; eating the sacrifice signified communion in the worship.

The Reformed Calvin notes the application: as eating the temple sacrifice united the Israelite to the altar and its God, so eating the idol-sacrifice would unite the Corinthian to the idol's altar — the very thing to be shunned. The verse displays the argument from Israel's altar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul points to "Israel after the flesh" (the Jewish nation in their temple worship) as a parallel (Barnes, Robertson); those who ate of the Jewish sacrifices were thereby "partakers of the altar," joined in fellowship with the altar and the God worshipped there, the eating signifying communion in the worship (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the application: as eating the temple sacrifice united the Israelite to the altar, so eating the idol-sacrifice would unite the Corinthian to the idol's altar. The verse displays the argument from Israel's altar. The verse marks the example: Israel after the flesh. The verse displays the principle: partakers of the altar. The verse displays the meaning: communion in the worship. The verse displays the application: to idol-feasts. The argument displays eating the sacrifice as communion with the altar. The verse displays the eating: of the sacrifices. The verse displays the partaking: of the altar. The verse displays the lesson: for idol-feasts. The argument from Israel's altar displays the principle that eating the sacrifice unites to the altar: as the Israelite who ate the temple sacrifice was a partaker of the altar and its worship, so eating the idol-sacrifice would join one to the idol's altar. The verse displays the analogy that condemns participation in idol-feasts, and clarifies what Paul does and does not affirm about idols (vv.19-20).
19:
"What say I then? that the idol is any thing, or that which is offered in sacrifice to idols is any thing?" The brief verse notes Paul guarding against misunderstanding about the idol. The grammatical voices note the question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul anticipates an objection: does he mean to say that the idol is really something (a true god), or that the meat offered to it is changed or has any real power? He had already taught (8:4) that an idol is nothing.

On the guard, the witnesses note the clarification. Barnes notes Paul is not retracting his earlier teaching that idols are nothing; he raises the question to prevent the inference that, by warning against idol-feasts, he attributes reality to idols.

The Reformed Calvin notes the balance: the idol in itself is nothing, yet (as the next verse shows) there is a real spiritual danger behind it; Paul holds both truths. The verse displays Paul guarding against misunderstanding about the idol.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul anticipates an objection — does he mean the idol is really something, or that the meat offered to it is changed or has power? He had already taught (8:4) that an idol is nothing (Barnes, Robertson); not retracting that teaching, he raises the question to prevent the inference that warning against idol-feasts attributes reality to idols (Barnes). Calvin notes the idol in itself is nothing, yet there is a real spiritual danger behind it; Paul holds both. The verse displays Paul guarding the point. The verse marks the question: is the idol anything? The verse displays the clarification: not retracting 8:4. The verse displays the balance: idol nothing, yet danger. The verse displays the purpose: preventing misunderstanding. The guard displays Paul holding the idol to be nothing. The verse displays the idol: nothing in itself. The verse displays the meat: unchanged. The verse displays the danger: behind it. Paul guarding against misunderstanding about the idol displays his careful balance: he does not mean that the idol is a real god or the sacrificial meat has power — he had taught that an idol is nothing — yet, as he will show, a real spiritual danger lurks behind idol-worship. The verse displays the careful distinction Paul preserves, and reveals the demonic reality behind idolatry (v.20).
20:
"But I say, that the things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to devils, and not to God: and I would not that ye should have fellowship with devils." The witnesses note that pagan sacrifices are offered to devils, with whom believers must have no fellowship. On "the things the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to devils," the grammatical voices note the reality. Barnes and Robertson note that though the idol is nothing, the pagan sacrifices are in fact offered "to devils" (daimonia, demons) — behind the lifeless idol stand real evil spirits, who are the true objects and beneficiaries of idol-worship.

On "and not to God," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes notes these sacrifices, whatever the worshippers suppose, are not offered to the true God but to demons — a real, dark spiritual transaction.

On "I would not that ye should have fellowship with devils," the Reformed Calvin notes the danger: to partake of the idol-feast is to enter into fellowship (communion) with demons; this is the real peril behind the seemingly indifferent meat. The verse displays that pagan sacrifices are offered to devils, with whom believers must have no fellowship.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: though the idol is nothing, the pagan sacrifices are in fact offered "to devils" (demons) — behind the lifeless idol stand real evil spirits, the true objects of idol-worship (Barnes, Robertson); these sacrifices, whatever the worshippers suppose, offered not to the true God but to demons (Barnes); so Paul would not have them "have fellowship with devils" — to partake of the idol-feast being to enter communion with demons (Calvin). The verse displays the demonic reality. The verse marks the reality: sacrifice to devils. The verse displays the contrast: not to God. The verse displays the danger: fellowship with devils. The verse displays the peril: behind the meat. The reality displays the demonic danger behind idolatry. The verse displays the devils: the true objects. The verse displays the fellowship: to be shunned. The verse displays the peril: communion with demons. That pagan sacrifices are offered to devils, with whom believers must have no fellowship, displays the real spiritual danger behind idolatry: though the idol is nothing, demons stand behind it, and to share the idol-feast is to enter fellowship with them. The verse displays the demonic reality that makes idol-feasts deadly, and presses the incompatibility of the two tables (v.21).
21:
"Ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord, and the cup of devils: ye cannot be partakers of the Lord's table, and of the table of devils." The witnesses note the incompatibility of the Lord's table and the table of devils. On "ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord, and the cup of devils," the grammatical voices note the exclusivity. Barnes and Robertson note Paul declares the two utterly incompatible: one cannot partake of the cup of the Lord (in the Supper, communion with Christ) and the cup of devils (in the idol-feast, fellowship with demons) — the two are mutually exclusive.

On "the Lord's table... the table of devils," the witnesses note the two communions. Barnes and Cyril note the Lord's table (the Supper) signifies communion with Christ; the table of devils (the idol-feast) signifies communion with demons; to share both is impossible — one cannot belong to Christ and to demons at once.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and eastern Cyril note the application: the believer must choose; partaking of the Lord's table binds him to Christ alone, forbidding any communion with the table of devils. The verse displays the incompatibility of the two tables.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul declares the two utterly incompatible — one cannot partake of the cup of the Lord (communion with Christ) and the cup of devils (fellowship with demons), the two mutually exclusive (Barnes, Robertson); the Lord's table signifying communion with Christ and the table of devils communion with demons, to share both being impossible (Barnes, Cyril). Wesley and Cyril note the believer must choose; the Lord's table binds him to Christ alone. The verse displays the two tables. The verse marks the exclusivity: cannot drink both cups. The verse displays the two communions: Christ or demons. The verse displays the impossibility: both at once. The verse displays the choice: Christ alone. The incompatibility displays the believer bound to Christ alone. The verse displays the cup: of the Lord. The verse displays the table: of devils. The verse displays the exclusion: of both. The incompatibility of the Lord's table and the table of devils displays the believer's exclusive allegiance to Christ: one cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of devils, nor share the Lord's table and the table of demons — the two communions cannot coexist. The verse displays the decisive incompatibility that forbids the idol-feast, and warns against provoking the Lord (v.22).
22:
"Do we provoke the Lord to jealousy? are we stronger than he?" The brief verse notes the warning against provoking the Lord's jealousy. The grammatical voices note the rebuke. Barnes and Robertson note Paul presses the point with two pointed questions: by sharing the idol-feast, "do we provoke the Lord to jealousy?" — stirring up his righteous jealousy, as Israel did (Deuteronomy 32:21), by giving to idols the devotion due to him alone.

On "are we stronger than he?" the witnesses note the folly. Barnes notes the second question exposes the folly: are we able to withstand God's power if we provoke him? To provoke the Lord while being utterly unable to resist his judgment is madness.

The Reformed Calvin notes the force: God is a jealous God who will not share his worship with idols; to provoke him is to court certain ruin, for none can stand against him. The verse displays the warning against provoking the Lord's jealousy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul presses the point with two questions — by sharing the idol-feast, do we provoke the Lord to jealousy (stirring his righteous jealousy, as Israel did, Deuteronomy 32:21, by giving idols the devotion due him alone)? (Barnes, Robertson); and "are we stronger than he?" exposing the folly — are we able to withstand his power if we provoke him? (Barnes). Calvin notes God is jealous and will not share his worship; to provoke him is to court certain ruin. The verse displays the warning against provoking the Lord. The verse marks the question: provoke to jealousy? The verse displays the folly: stronger than he? The verse displays the jealousy: God's righteous claim. The verse displays the madness: provoking the irresistible. The warning displays the peril of provoking a jealous God. The verse displays the jealousy: provoked. The verse displays the weakness: ours. The verse displays the folly: resisting God. The warning against provoking the Lord's jealousy displays the madness of the idol-feast: to share it is to provoke the jealous God who claims our worship alone — and to do so while utterly unable to withstand his power. The verse displays the decisive close of the argument against idolatry, and turns to the principle of edifying liberty (vv.23-24).
23:
"All things are lawful for me, but all things are not expedient: all things are lawful for me, but all things edify not." The witnesses note that liberty is limited by expediency and edification. On "all things are lawful for me, but all things are not expedient," the grammatical voices note the qualification. Barnes and Robertson note Paul takes up the Corinthian maxim "all things are lawful" (perhaps their slogan) and qualifies it: even of things truly indifferent and lawful, not all are "expedient" (profitable, helpful) — lawfulness is not the only consideration.

On "all things edify not," the witnesses note the second limit. Barnes and Calvin note the further qualification: not all lawful things "edify" (build up the church, benefit others); the believer's liberty must be governed by what builds up, not merely by what is permitted.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the principle: Christian liberty is real but not absolute; it is to be limited by the good of others and the edification of the church, not used selfishly. The verse displays that liberty is limited by expediency and edification.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul takes up the Corinthian maxim "all things are lawful" and qualifies it — even of things truly indifferent, not all are "expedient" (profitable, helpful), lawfulness not the only consideration (Barnes, Robertson); and not all lawful things "edify" (build up the church, benefit others), liberty to be governed by what builds up (Barnes, Calvin). Henry and Calvin note Christian liberty is real but not absolute, to be limited by the good of others and the edification of the church. The verse displays liberty limited. The verse marks the maxim: all things lawful. The verse displays the first limit: not expedient. The verse displays the second limit: edify not. The verse displays the principle: liberty governed by good. Liberty displays the limits of expediency and edification. The verse displays the lawful: not all helpful. The verse displays the edifying: the standard. The verse displays the governance: by others' good. That liberty is limited by expediency and edification displays the governing principle of Christian freedom: though all indifferent things are lawful, not all are profitable or build up the church, so liberty is to be measured not by what is permitted but by what helps and edifies. The verse displays the qualification of Christian liberty, and states its great rule of seeking others' good (v.24).
24:
"Let no man seek his own, but every man another's wealth." The brief verse notes the rule of seeking another's good, not one's own. The grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note the governing rule of Christian liberty: let no one seek merely his own (his own interest, advantage, gratification), but each seek "another's wealth" (the other's good, welfare, benefit) — the law of unselfish love.

On "another's wealth," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes notes "wealth" here means well-being, profit, good — not riches; the believer is to consult the spiritual and temporal good of others, not only his own.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the application: this is the heart of the whole matter of liberty and idol-meats — self is to yield to the good of the brother; love seeks the neighbour's edification above its own rights. The verse displays the rule of seeking another's good.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the governing rule — let no one seek merely his own interest or gratification, but each seek "another's wealth" (the other's good, welfare, benefit), the law of unselfish love (Barnes, Robertson); "wealth" meaning well-being and profit, not riches, the believer consulting others' good, not only his own (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note this is the heart of the matter: self yields to the brother's good, love seeking the neighbour's edification above its own rights. The verse displays the rule of seeking another's good. The verse marks the negation: not his own. The verse displays the rule: another's wealth. The verse displays the meaning: the other's good. The verse displays the principle: unselfish love. The rule displays love seeking the neighbour's good above self. The verse displays the self: denied. The verse displays the other: sought. The verse displays the love: unselfish. The rule of seeking another's good, not one's own, displays the heart of Christian liberty rightly used: the believer is not to consult his own advantage but the welfare of others, love subordinating his own rights to the good and edification of his neighbour. The verse displays the law of love that governs the use of liberty, and applies it concretely to buying and eating meat (vv.25-30).
25:
"Whatsoever is sold in the shambles, that eat, asking no question for conscience sake:" The witnesses note the freedom to eat market meat without scruple. On "whatsoever is sold in the shambles, that eat," the grammatical voices note the liberty. Barnes and Robertson note Paul grants real liberty: whatever meat is sold in the "shambles" (the public meat-market, where much had been offered to idols) may be eaten freely — the believer need not avoid it.

On "asking no question for conscience sake," the witnesses note the freedom from scruple. Barnes and Chrysostom note the believer is to eat "asking no question" — not making anxious inquiry whether the meat had been offered to an idol; to do so would needlessly burden the conscience, since the meat itself is indifferent.

The Reformed Calvin notes the balance: in itself the meat is clean, and the believer's liberty is real; the scruple arises only when conscience is involved (v.28). The verse displays the freedom to eat market meat without scruple.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul grants real liberty — whatever meat is sold in the "shambles" (the public meat-market, where much had been offered to idols) may be eaten freely (Barnes, Robertson); the believer to eat "asking no question," not making anxious inquiry whether it had been offered to an idol, since the meat itself is indifferent and to inquire would needlessly burden the conscience (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes the meat is clean and the liberty real, the scruple arising only when conscience is involved. The verse displays the freedom to eat market meat. The verse marks the liberty: eat what is sold. The verse displays the freedom: asking no question. The verse displays the reason: the meat indifferent. The verse displays the balance: real liberty. The freedom displays the believer's liberty with market meat. The verse displays the shambles: the market. The verse displays the eating: freely. The verse displays the conscience: unburdened. The freedom to eat market meat without scruple displays the real liberty the believer enjoys: meat sold in the market, even if once offered to idols, may be eaten freely without anxious inquiry, for the meat in itself is indifferent. The verse displays the proper use of liberty in private eating, and grounds it in God's ownership of all things (v.26).
26:
"For the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof." The brief verse grounds the liberty in God's ownership of all things. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul grounds the freedom to eat in Psalm 24:1: "the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof" — all the produce of the earth belongs to God, who gives it for human use.

On the inference, the witnesses note the cleanness of all. Barnes and Calvin note that since all things are God's and given by him, the meat is clean and good in itself; an idol's claim cannot defile what belongs to the Lord and is received with thanksgiving.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: God's ownership sanctifies the use of his creatures; the believer may freely enjoy what God has made and given, the idol having no real power to pollute it. The verse displays the liberty grounded in God's ownership of all things.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul grounds the freedom to eat in Psalm 24:1 — "the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof," all the produce of the earth belonging to God, who gives it for human use (Barnes, Robertson); so the meat is clean and good in itself, an idol's claim unable to defile what belongs to the Lord and is received with thanks (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes God's ownership sanctifies the use of his creatures. The verse displays the liberty grounded in God's ownership. The verse marks the citation: the earth is the Lord's. The verse displays the inference: all clean. The verse displays the reason: God's gift. The verse displays the principle: ownership sanctifies use. The ground displays God's ownership making all clean. The verse displays the earth: the Lord's. The verse displays the fulness: his. The verse displays the cleanness: of all. The liberty grounded in God's ownership of all things displays the basis of the believer's freedom: since the earth and all its fulness belong to the Lord and are given for use, the meat is clean and good, and no idol's claim can defile what God has made and bestowed. The verse displays the divine ownership that frees the conscience, and extends the liberty to an unbeliever's table (v.27).
27:
"If any of them that believe not bid you to a feast, and ye be disposed to go; whatsoever is set before you, eat, asking no question for conscience sake." The witnesses note the liberty to eat at an unbeliever's table. On "if any of them that believe not bid you to a feast," the grammatical voices note the case. Barnes and Robertson note Paul addresses the social case: if an unbeliever invites a Christian to a meal, and he is willing to go (Paul does not forbid such friendly social intercourse), he may attend.

On "whatsoever is set before you, eat, asking no question," the witnesses note the freedom. Barnes notes the same rule as for the market (v.25): eat whatever is served without anxious inquiry about its origin — the meat being indifferent, the conscience need not be troubled.

On "for conscience sake," the eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the balance: the liberty holds so long as no question of conscience is raised; the next verse provides the exception when someone points out the meat as idol-offered. The verse displays the liberty to eat at an unbeliever's table.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul addresses the social case — if an unbeliever invites a Christian to a meal and he is willing to go (such friendly intercourse not forbidden), he may attend (Barnes, Robertson); and eat whatever is served without anxious inquiry about its origin, as with market meat, the meat being indifferent (Barnes). Cyril and Calvin note the liberty holds so long as no question of conscience is raised, the next verse providing the exception. The verse displays the liberty at an unbeliever's table. The verse marks the case: an unbeliever's invitation. The verse displays the freedom: eat what is set. The verse displays the rule: asking no question. The verse displays the balance: until conscience intervenes. The liberty displays free eating at the unbeliever's table. The verse displays the feast: an unbeliever's. The verse displays the eating: freely. The verse displays the conscience: unburdened. The liberty to eat at an unbeliever's table displays the believer's freedom in ordinary social life: invited by an unbeliever, the Christian may go and eat whatever is set before him without anxious inquiry, the meat being indifferent. The verse displays the freedom that governs friendly intercourse, immediately bounded by regard for conscience (vv.28-29).
28:
"But if any man say unto you, This is offered in sacrifice unto idols, eat not for his sake that shewed it, and for conscience sake: for the earth is the Lord's, and the fulness thereof:" The witnesses note the exception: abstain when the meat is pointed out as idol-offered. On "if any man say, This is offered in sacrifice unto idols," the grammatical voices note the case. Barnes and Robertson note the exception to the liberty: if someone present (perhaps a weak brother, or a scrupulous heathen) expressly points out that the meat had been offered to an idol, the believer is to change his course.

On "eat not for his sake that shewed it, and for conscience sake," the witnesses note the abstention. Barnes notes the believer is then to abstain — not because the meat is in itself defiling, but "for his sake" (the one who pointed it out) and for conscience' sake; lest he wound the weak conscience or seem to countenance idolatry.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the principle: love yields its liberty when its exercise would harm another's conscience; the repeated "the earth is the Lord's" (in some texts) reaffirms that the abstention is not from any defilement in the meat. The verse displays the exception: abstain when the meat is pointed out.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the exception to the liberty — if someone present (a weak brother or scrupulous heathen) expressly points out that the meat had been offered to an idol, the believer is to abstain (Barnes, Robertson); not because the meat defiles, but "for his sake" (the one who pointed it out) and for conscience' sake, lest he wound the weak conscience or seem to countenance idolatry (Barnes). Wesley notes love yields its liberty when its exercise would harm another's conscience. The verse displays the exception. The verse marks the case: the meat pointed out. The verse displays the abstention: eat not. The verse displays the reason: for his sake. The verse displays the principle: love yields liberty. The exception displays liberty yielded for the other's conscience. The verse displays the pointing-out: of the meat. The verse displays the abstaining: in love. The verse displays the regard: for conscience. The exception — abstain when the meat is pointed out as idol-offered — displays the limit love sets on liberty: though the meat is indifferent, the believer is to refrain for the sake of the one who pointed it out and for conscience, lest he wound the weak or seem to endorse idolatry. The verse displays liberty surrendered for another's good, and explains whose conscience is in view (v.29).
29:
"Conscience, I say, not thine own, but of the other: for why is my liberty judged of another man's conscience?" The witnesses note that it is the other's conscience, not one's own, that limits liberty. On "conscience, not thine own, but of the other," the grammatical voices note the clarification. Barnes and Robertson note Paul clarifies which conscience he means: not the eating believer's own conscience (which is free, knowing the meat indifferent) but the conscience of "the other" (the weak brother or the observer) — it is his scruple, not the eater's, that calls for abstention.

On "why is my liberty judged of another's conscience?" the witnesses note the question. Barnes notes Paul anticipates an objection: why should my real liberty be condemned or limited by another's scruple? The answer (vv.31-33) is the law of love and the glory of God — liberty is freely surrendered, not destroyed.

The Reformed Calvin notes the balance: the believer's own conscience remains free; he yields his liberty voluntarily for love's sake, not because the other's scruple binds him in truth. The verse displays that the other's conscience, not one's own, limits liberty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul clarifies which conscience he means — not the eating believer's own (which is free, knowing the meat indifferent) but the conscience of "the other" (the weak brother or observer); it is his scruple, not the eater's, that calls for abstention (Barnes, Robertson); and Paul anticipates the objection, "why should my liberty be judged by another's conscience?" answered by the law of love and the glory of God (Barnes). Calvin notes the believer's own conscience remains free, yielding voluntarily for love. The verse displays the other's conscience limiting liberty. The verse marks the clarification: not thine own. The verse displays the limit: the other's conscience. The verse displays the question: why judged? The verse displays the answer: love, freely yielded. The clarification displays liberty yielded, not bound, for the other. The verse displays the conscience: the other's. The verse displays the freedom: one's own. The verse displays the yielding: voluntary. That it is the other's conscience, not one's own, that limits liberty displays the voluntary nature of the surrender: the believer's own conscience remains free, but he abstains for the sake of the other's scruple — his liberty not truly bound, but freely yielded in love. The verse displays the freedom preserved even in self-denial, and points to the great governing aim (vv.31-33).
30:
"For if I by grace be a partaker, why am I evil spoken of for that for which I give thanks?" The brief verse notes the believer's thankful enjoyment of God's gifts. The grammatical voices note the question. Barnes and Robertson note Paul continues the thought: if he partakes of food "by grace" (with thankfulness, as God's gift, or by God's favour), why should he be evil spoken of (blamed, reproached) for eating that for which he gives thanks?

On "for that for which I give thanks," the witnesses note the sanctifying thanksgiving. Barnes notes the food received with thanksgiving is sanctified to the believer (1 Timothy 4:4-5); his thankful enjoyment of God's gift should not expose him to reproach.

The Reformed Calvin notes the balance: this confirms the believer's real liberty — yet (in harmony with vv.28-29) he will still forgo it where its use would give offence; thankfulness sanctifies the gift, but love governs its use. The verse displays the believer's thankful enjoyment of God's gifts.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: if Paul partakes "by grace" (with thankfulness, as God's gift), why should he be evil spoken of for eating that for which he gives thanks? (Barnes, Robertson); the food received with thanksgiving being sanctified to the believer (1 Timothy 4:4-5), his thankful enjoyment not deserving reproach (Barnes). Calvin notes this confirms the real liberty, yet he still forgoes it where it would offend; thankfulness sanctifies the gift, love governs its use. The verse displays the thankful enjoyment of God's gifts. The verse marks the partaking: by grace. The verse displays the question: why reproached? The verse displays the sanctifying: thanksgiving. The verse displays the balance: liberty yet love. The enjoyment displays the gift sanctified by thanksgiving. The verse displays the grace: in partaking. The verse displays the thanks: sanctifying. The verse displays the liberty: real. The believer's thankful enjoyment of God's gifts displays the reality of his liberty: partaking by grace and with thanksgiving, he eats what God has sanctified to him and should not be reproached for it — though, in love, he will still forgo it where its use would offend. The verse displays the freedom that thanksgiving secures, and rises to the great governing principle (v.31).
31:
"Whether therefore ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God." The witnesses note the great principle: do all to the glory of God. On "whether ye eat, or drink," the grammatical voices note the application. Barnes and Robertson note Paul gathers the whole discussion of meats and liberty into one grand principle: even in the most ordinary acts — eating and drinking — and indeed in "whatsoever ye do," the believer is to act with a view to God's glory.

On "do all to the glory of God," the witnesses note the comprehensive rule. Barnes and Calvin note this is the supreme and universal standard of the Christian life: every act, great or small, is to be done so as to honour God, to promote his glory — the single aim that resolves all questions of liberty and conscience.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the breadth: nothing is too small to be consecrated to God's glory; this principle, rightly applied, governs the use of all liberty and sanctifies the whole of life. The verse displays the great principle: do all to the glory of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul gathers the whole discussion into one grand principle — even in eating and drinking, and in "whatsoever ye do," the believer is to act with a view to God's glory (Barnes, Robertson); this the supreme and universal standard of the Christian life, every act to be done so as to honour God, the single aim that resolves all questions of liberty and conscience (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note nothing is too small to be consecrated to God's glory; the principle sanctifies the whole of life. The verse displays the great principle. The verse marks the application: eat or drink. The verse displays the scope: whatsoever ye do. The verse displays the standard: the glory of God. The verse displays the breadth: all of life. The principle displays God's glory as the aim of every act. The verse displays the eating: to God's glory. The verse displays the doing: all things. The verse displays the aim: God honoured. The great principle — whether ye eat or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God — displays the supreme rule that governs the whole of the Christian life: every act, even the most ordinary, is to be done so as to honour God, the single aim that resolves every question of liberty. The verse displays the glory of God as the comprehensive standard, and applies it to giving no offence (vv.32-33).
32:
"Give none offence, neither to the Jews, nor to the Gentiles, nor to the church of God:" The brief verse notes the rule of giving no offence to any. The grammatical voices note the threefold scope. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the glory of God to conduct toward others: give "none offence" (no occasion of stumbling or scandal) to anyone — and names three classes: the Jews, the Gentiles (Greeks), and the church of God.

On "none offence," the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes notes "offence" means a stumbling-block, anything that would needlessly hinder another's salvation or wound the church; the believer is to avoid giving such occasion to any group.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the breadth: the believer's regard for others extends to all — unbelieving Jew and Gentile (lest they be repelled from the gospel) and fellow Christians (lest they stumble); love consults the good of all. The verse displays the rule of giving no offence to any.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul applies the glory of God to conduct toward others — give "none offence" (no occasion of stumbling or scandal) to anyone, naming three classes: the Jews, the Gentiles, and the church of God (Barnes, Robertson); "offence" meaning a stumbling-block, anything that would needlessly hinder another's salvation or wound the church (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the regard extends to all — unbelieving Jew and Gentile (lest repelled from the gospel) and fellow Christians (lest they stumble). The verse displays giving no offence. The verse marks the rule: none offence. The verse displays the scope: Jews, Gentiles, church. The verse displays the meaning: no stumbling-block. The verse displays the breadth: regard for all. The rule displays love avoiding offence to every group. The verse displays the offence: avoided. The verse displays the classes: three. The verse displays the regard: for all. The rule of giving no offence to any displays the outward bearing of the glory of God: the believer is to put no stumbling-block before Jew, Gentile, or the church, lest he needlessly hinder another's salvation or wound a fellow Christian. The verse displays the universal regard love requires, and grounds it in Paul's own example (v.33).
33:
"Even as I please all men in all things, not seeking mine own profit, but the profit of many, that they may be saved." The witnesses note Paul's own example of self-denying love for others' salvation. On "even as I please all men in all things," the grammatical voices note the example. Barnes and Robertson note Paul offers himself as the pattern: he seeks to please (accommodate, conciliate) all men in all things — not from man-pleasing flattery, but adapting himself for their good, as he had described (9:19-22).

On "not seeking mine own profit, but the profit of many," the witnesses note the self-denial. Barnes and Calvin note the key: Paul does not consult his own advantage but the profit (benefit, good) of the many — subordinating his own interest and liberty to others' welfare.

On "that they may be saved," the Reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the supreme aim: the great end of all this self-accommodation is the salvation of others — that they may be saved; love bends every liberty toward this highest good. The verse displays Paul's example of self-denying love for others' salvation.

The witnesses agree: Paul offers himself as the pattern — he seeks to please (accommodate, conciliate) all men in all things, not from flattery but adapting himself for their good, as in 9:19-22 (Barnes, Robertson); not consulting his own advantage but the profit of the many, subordinating his interest and liberty to others' welfare (Barnes, Calvin); the supreme aim being their salvation — "that they may be saved," love bending every liberty toward this highest good (Calvin). The verse displays Paul's example. The verse marks the pattern: please all men. The verse displays the self-denial: not his own profit. The verse displays the aim: that they may be saved. The verse displays the love: for others' good. The example displays self-denying love aimed at others' salvation. The verse displays the pleasing: of all. The verse displays the profit: of many. The verse displays the salvation: the aim. Paul's example of self-denying love for others' salvation displays the crown of the whole discussion: seeking not his own profit but the good of the many, he accommodates himself to all for the supreme end that they may be saved — the law of love bending every liberty toward the salvation of others. The verse displays the apostle's pattern of sacrificial love, sealing the great principle of using liberty for the glory of God and the good of all.
